title: Sketching, Datums & Extrude Features
description: Master datum features, sketch creation, sketch geometry, feature-based modeling, parent-child relationships, dimensions, constraints, and extrude feature options.

topics.title: Datum Features & Sketch Creation

content.text: Every feature in Creo starts with reference geometry — datums — and most features require a sketch. Understanding how to select and create datum features and how to initiate a sketch is the foundation of feature creation.
Datum Feature Types

rows: Datum Plane
Through points/edges, offset from surface, through axis at angle
Sketch planes, mirror planes, measurement references, assembly constraints
Datum Axis
Through edges, intersection of planes, through cylinder center
Revolution axis, pattern axis, measurement reference
Datum Point
On surface, on vertex, at coordinates, on curve
Measurement reference, coordinate system origin, assembly constraint point
Datum Coordinate System
Three planes, three points, or by reference
Part origin, assembly reference, measurement datum for analysis

content.text: Starting a Sketch

cards.back: The sketch plane determines the 2D surface on which you draw your sketch. Choose a datum plane (RIGHT, TOP, FRONT) or any flat model surface. The sketch plane defines the orientation of the feature cross-section.

After selecting the sketch plane, specify which direction is 'up' by choosing a reference surface and its orientation (Top, Bottom, Left, Right). This ensures the sketch appears in the expected orientation on screen.

Sketch references are existing edges or datums that your sketch dimensions are measured from. Creo selects default references, but you can add or change them. References determine how the sketch is positioned relative to the existing model.

content.text: Choose sketch planes that align with the most natural view of the feature cross-section. For a tabletop, the TOP datum plane is natural because you're looking down at the flat surface.

keyTakeaways: Datum planes, axes, points, and coordinate systems provide reference geometry for feature creation
Every sketch requires selecting a sketch plane, setting orientation, and defining references
Default datum planes (RIGHT, TOP, FRONT) are often used as initial sketch planes
Sketch references determine how sketch dimensions are measured from existing geometry

topics.title: Sketch Geometry & Rectangles

content.text: The Sketcher environment provides tools for creating 2D geometry that defines the cross-section of features. Rectangles are one of the most common sketch shapes, used for extrusions like the belt sander tabletop.
Sketch Geometry Tools

rows: Line
Creates straight line segments between two points
Irregular polygons, open profiles, construction geometry
Rectangle (Corner)
Creates a rectangle from two diagonal corners
Rectangular extrusions when one corner is at a known reference point
Rectangle (Center)
Creates a rectangle from center point and one corner
Rectangular extrusions centered on a reference point
Circle
Creates a circle from center point and radius
Cylindrical features, holes, circular pads
Arc
Creates circular arcs (3-point, center+ends, tangent)
Rounded corners in sketches, curved profiles
Centerline
Creates a construction line used for symmetry and revolution
Mirror axis, revolution axis, symmetry constraint reference

content.text: Construction geometry (like centerlines) is not part of the final sketch profile. It exists only as a reference for dimensions, constraints, and symmetry operations. Toggle construction mode with the Construction icon in the Sketcher ribbon.

content.prompt: Match each sketch tool to its best use case:

pairs.right: Creating a tabletop sketch centered on the origin
Establishing a symmetry axis for mirror constraints
Creating a cross-section for a cylindrical roller
Adding a smooth transition between two lines

keyTakeaways: The Sketcher provides tools for lines, rectangles, circles, arcs, and construction geometry
Rectangles are created from corners or center — choose based on your reference point
Centerlines and construction geometry serve as references but aren't part of the final profile
Choose the sketch tool that best matches the shape of the feature cross-section you need

topics.title: Feature-Based Modeling & Parent-Child

content.text: In Creo Parametric, every model is built by adding features one at a time. Each feature references existing geometry, creating parent-child dependencies that are critical to understand for successful modeling.
Feature-Based Modeling Principles

Features are the building blocks of a Creo model. Each feature adds or removes material, and together they define the complete 3D shape. The order of feature creation matters because later features depend on earlier ones.

cards.back: The first solid feature in a model, typically the largest or most fundamental shape. All subsequent features reference the base feature directly or indirectly. For the belt sander tabletop, the base feature is a rectangular extrude.

A feature that provides references (surfaces, edges, datums) used by other features. If a parent is modified, all its children update. If a parent is deleted, its children may fail. Example: the base extrude is the parent of a cutout sketched on its surface.

A feature that depends on references from a parent feature. Children automatically update when parents change. A round applied to an edge of an extrude is a child of that extrude — it depends on the edge existing.

Features form chains of dependencies: Feature A → Feature B → Feature C. Changing Feature A affects B and C. Deleting Feature A may fail both B and C. Understanding the chain helps you plan your model for maximum flexibility.

content.text: Deleting a parent feature removes the references that child features depend on, which can cause them to fail. Before deleting any feature, use the Reference Viewer (Edit > Investigate > Reference Viewer) to check for dependents.
Managing Parent-Child Relationships
You can reroute parent-child references to different geometry if you need to restructure the model. This allows you to change which features depend on which, without losing the child features.

content.prompt: Arrange these features in a logical creation order for a belt sander tabletop:
items: Add cutout extrude for belt slot

keyTakeaways: Models are built feature-by-feature, with each feature adding or removing material
Parent features provide references that child features depend on
Deleting a parent can cause all its children to fail — always check references first
Plan your feature creation order carefully to create a logical parent-child hierarchy

topics.title: Dimensions, Constraints & Centerlines

content.text: Sketches in Creo are controlled by dimensions and constraints. Together, they fully define the size, shape, and position of sketch geometry. A fully constrained sketch is essential for predictable feature behavior.
Dimension Types

cards.back: Measures the distance between two points, a point and a line, or two parallel lines. Created by selecting two references and placing the dimension text. Used for lengths, widths, and offsets.
Measures the radius or diameter of circles and arcs. Select the circle or arc to create the dimension. Toggle between radius and diameter display as needed.
Measures the angle between two lines or between a line and a reference direction. Select two lines to create the dimension. Used for tapered or angled features.

A driven (read-only) dimension calculated from other dimensions and constraints. Cannot be directly modified — it shows the resulting value. Useful for verification without over-constraining the sketch.

content.text: Sketch Constraints

rows: Horizontal
H
Forces a line to be horizontal (parallel to the sketch X-axis)
Vertical
V
Forces a line to be vertical (parallel to the sketch Y-axis)
Perpendicular
Forces two lines to meet at a 90° angle
Tangent
T
Forces a line and arc (or two arcs) to meet smoothly without a sharp corner
Equal
L=
Forces two lines or arcs to have the same length or radius
Symmetric
Forces two points or entities to be symmetric about a centerline
Coincident
Forces a point to lie exactly on a line, arc, or another point

content.text: Centerlines for Symmetry

Centerlines are construction geometry used to define axes of symmetry. When you add a symmetric constraint referencing a centerline, geometry on both sides remains mirrored. Centerlines are also used as revolution axes for revolve features.

A fully constrained sketch has no degrees of freedom remaining — every entity's position and size is determined by dimensions and constraints. Creo shows the constraint status in the Sketcher status bar. An under-constrained sketch can move unexpectedly during regeneration.

keyTakeaways: Dimensions control size (linear, radial, angular); constraints control shape and position
A fully constrained sketch has zero degrees of freedom and behaves predictably
Centerlines establish symmetry axes and revolution axes
Constraints like horizontal, vertical, symmetric, and equal reduce the number of dimensions needed

topics.title: Extrude Feature Options

content.text: The Extrude feature is the most commonly used feature in Creo Parametric. It creates 3D geometry by projecting a 2D sketch profile along a direction perpendicular to the sketch plane. Multiple depth and direction options control the final shape.
Depth Types

rows: Blind
Extrude to a specified numeric depth value
When you know the exact depth dimension required
Symmetric
Extrude equally on both sides of the sketch plane
When the feature should be centered on the sketch plane
Through All
Extrude through the entire existing model geometry
When the feature (often a cut) must pass completely through the part
Through Next
Extrude until reaching the next surface
When the depth should stop at the first surface encountered
To Selected
Extrude to a specific surface, plane, or point
When the depth must end at a specific reference geometry

content.text: Direction & Material Options

cards.back: By default, extrudes go in one direction from the sketch plane. You can flip the direction or choose 'Both Sides' to extrude in both directions (with potentially different depths on each side). The direction arrow on the preview shows which way the extrude will go.
The default mode for extrudes — adds solid material to create new geometry. Used for creating the base feature and adding material to an existing part. The extrude creates a solid protrusion.
Creates a cut or pocket by removing material from the existing solid. Used for slots, holes, grooves, and cutouts. The sketch defines the cross-section of material to be removed.

content.text: For your first feature (base feature), only Add Material is available since there is no existing geometry to cut. For all subsequent features, you can choose between Add and Remove Material based on your design intent.

content.prompt: Match each depth type to the correct scenario:

pairs.right: Creating a tabletop with a specific 10mm thickness
Creating a rib centered on a datum plane
Cutting a slot that must go completely through the part
Extending an extrude to meet a specific angled surface

keyTakeaways: Extrude is the most common feature — it projects a 2D sketch into 3D geometry
Five depth types (Blind, Symmetric, Through All, Through Next, To Selected) control how far the extrude extends
Direction can be one side, other side, or both sides of the sketch plane
Add Material creates protrusions; Remove Material creates cuts and pockets

topics.title: Exercise: Create Belt Sander Tabletop

content.text: In this exercise, you will create the belt sander tabletop from scratch using datum planes, sketches, and extrude features. This is the first part you will model in the belt sander assembly.
Start a new part file for this exercise. Set your working directory to the exercise folder and create a new file named TABLETOP.PRT.

content.title: Create the Belt Sander Tabletop
content.objective: Create a new part file and build the belt sander tabletop using extrude features with proper sketch planes, dimensions, and constraints.

steps.action: Create a new part file: File > New > Part > Solid. Name it TABLETOP.
steps.detail: Creating a new part opens a blank model with the three default datum planes (RIGHT, TOP, FRONT) and coordinate system. These datums will serve as your initial sketch references.
steps.hint: Make sure 'Use default template' is checked, or select the appropriate part template.

steps.action: Select the FRONT datum plane as the sketch plane, then click the Extrude tool on the Model tab.
steps.detail: The FRONT datum plane provides a view looking at the front face of the tabletop. Selecting the sketch plane first and then the feature tool is the most common workflow.
steps.hint: You can also start the Extrude tool first and then select the sketch plane when prompted.

steps.action: In the Sketcher, draw a center rectangle centered on the origin. Dimension it to 180mm wide and 100mm tall.
steps.detail: The center rectangle is centered on the origin point where the three default datum planes intersect. This ensures the tabletop is centered on the part coordinate system.
steps.hint: Use Sketch > Rectangle > Center Rectangle. Click the origin first, then click to set the corner.

steps.action: Add dimensions: 180mm for width, 100mm for height. Ensure the sketch is fully constrained (no degrees of freedom).
steps.detail: Double-click existing dimension values to modify them. The status bar should show 'Fully Constrained' or 'Section is valid' when all degrees of freedom are resolved.
steps.hint: If extra dimensions appear, delete the unwanted ones before adding your desired dimensions.

steps.action: Close the Sketcher (click the checkmark), then set the extrude depth to Blind, 12mm. Accept the feature.
steps.detail: Blind depth of 12mm creates the tabletop thickness. The extrude direction should point away from the sketch plane. Flip the direction if needed using the direction arrow.
steps.hint: The depth value field is in the feature dashboard that appears when the Sketcher closes.

steps.action: Spin the model using the middle mouse button to inspect all sides of the tabletop.
steps.detail: Visual inspection confirms the extrude created the expected rectangular solid. Check that the dimensions look proportional (wider than it is deep, with a thin profile).

steps.action: Check the Model Tree to verify the feature was created correctly. You should see the default datums and one Extrude feature.
steps.detail: The Model Tree should show: RIGHT, TOP, FRONT (datum planes), PRT_CSYS_DEF (coordinate system), and Extrude 1 (your base feature).

steps.action: Use Edit Dimensions on the extrude to verify the values are 180mm × 100mm × 12mm.
steps.detail: Edit Dimensions displays the actual dimension values on the model. This is the quickest way to verify feature dimensions match your design intent.

steps.action: Save the part (Ctrl+S).
steps.detail: Saving creates the first version of the file (TABLETOP.PRT.1) in your working directory.

keyTakeaways: Start new parts with File > New > Part > Solid
Select the sketch plane before starting the feature tool
Center rectangles on the origin to keep the part centered on the coordinate system
Always verify dimensions after feature creation using Edit Dimensions

topics.title: Exercise: Create Cutout & Slider

content.text: In this exercise, you will add a cutout to the tabletop and create the slider part. These exercises demonstrate using extrudes with Remove Material and creating a second part.
Continue with the TABLETOP.PRT file from the previous exercise. You will also create a new SLIDER.PRT file.

content.title: Create Cutout and Slider Parts
content.objective: Add a material-removal extrude to the tabletop and create the slider as a new part.

steps.action: Open TABLETOP.PRT (if not already open). Select the top surface of the tabletop as the sketch plane.
steps.detail: Using the top surface (instead of a datum plane) as the sketch plane places the sketch directly on the face where the cut will start. This creates a parent-child relationship between the cutout and the base extrude.
steps.hint: Click the top flat surface to select it before starting the Extrude tool.

steps.action: Start the Extrude tool. In the Sketcher, draw a center rectangle for the cutout slot. Dimension it to 120mm long and 25mm wide, centered on the origin.
steps.detail: The cutout creates a slot through the tabletop for the sanding belt. Centering it on the origin aligns it with the tabletop center.

steps.action: Close the Sketcher. In the feature dashboard, select Remove Material and set depth to Through All.
steps.detail: Remove Material cuts into the existing solid instead of adding material. Through All ensures the slot goes completely through the tabletop regardless of its thickness.
steps.hint: Look for the material removal icon (a block with a minus sign) in the feature dashboard.

steps.action: Accept the feature and verify the cutout appears as a slot through the tabletop.
steps.detail: The tabletop should now have a rectangular slot through its center. Spin the model to verify the cut goes completely through.

steps.action: Create a new part file named SLIDER.PRT. Select the FRONT datum plane as the sketch plane.
steps.detail: The slider is a separate part that will fit into the tabletop cutout. Creating it as a separate file allows it to be used in the assembly later.

steps.action: Draw a center rectangle and dimension it to 115mm long and 20mm wide.
steps.detail: The slider is slightly smaller than the cutout (115mm vs 120mm, 20mm vs 25mm) to allow clearance for the sanding belt path.

steps.action: Set the extrude depth to Blind, 8mm. Accept and save.
steps.detail: The slider is thinner than the tabletop to allow it to move freely within the cutout channel.

keyTakeaways: Remove Material extrudes create cuts, slots, and pockets in existing geometry
Through All depth ensures cuts go completely through the part
Using a model surface as a sketch plane creates a parent-child relationship
Related parts should be dimensioned relative to each other for proper fit

topics.title: Exercise: Create Slot

content.text: In this exercise, you will add a slot feature to the slider part using a combination of sketching techniques including centerlines for symmetry.
Continue with the SLIDER.PRT file from the previous exercise.

content.title: Create a Slot in the Slider
content.objective: Add a symmetric slot feature to the slider using centerlines and remove-material extrude.

steps.action: Open SLIDER.PRT. Select the top surface as the sketch plane and start the Extrude tool.
steps.detail: The slot will be cut into the top face of the slider to create a channel for the sanding belt adjustment mechanism.

steps.action: In the Sketcher, draw a horizontal centerline through the origin.
steps.detail: The centerline establishes a symmetry axis. The slot will be symmetric about this line, ensuring it is centered on the slider.
steps.hint: Use Sketch > Line > Centerline, or toggle the line tool to construction mode.

steps.action: Draw a slot profile using lines and arcs: two parallel lines with semicircular arcs at each end. Dimension the slot to 80mm long and 10mm wide.
steps.detail: The slot profile consists of two horizontal lines connected by two 180° arcs. This creates a smooth-ended slot shape that is stronger than a rectangular cutout.
steps.hint: Draw the two horizontal lines first, then add tangent arcs at the ends to connect them.

steps.action: Add a Symmetric constraint to make the slot symmetric about the centerline.
steps.detail: The symmetric constraint ensures the slot remains centered on the slider regardless of dimension changes. This captures the design intent of a centered slot.
steps.hint: Select the symmetric constraint tool, then click two corresponding points and the centerline.

steps.action: Close the Sketcher. Set Remove Material and Symmetric depth with a total depth of 5mm. Accept the feature.
steps.detail: Symmetric depth with Remove Material creates a centered channel in the slider. The slot will extend 2.5mm from each side of the sketch plane.

steps.action: Save the part and verify the slot feature in the Model Tree and graphics area.
steps.detail: The Model Tree should now show two extrude features: the base extrude and the slot. Spin the model to inspect the slot from all angles.

keyTakeaways: Centerlines establish symmetry axes for sketch constraints
Slot profiles combine lines and arcs for smooth-ended openings
Symmetric constraints capture design intent for centered features
Symmetric depth with Remove Material creates centered channels
